text: We present the case of a seventy-year-old female with near-circumferential duodenal laterally spreading lesion involving the major papilla. The patient was referred for endoscopic mucosal resection of this lesion. She had severe medical comorbidities precluding surgery, including severe chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, rheumatoid arthritis, and factor V-laden mutation on warfarin.

Under general anesthesia, the surgeon uses a duodenoscope to perform a complete resection of the laterally spreading duodenal lesion. The chromogelofusion endoscopic mucosal resection (EMR) technique is applied sequentially, followed by an on-block endoscopic papulectomy.

The surgeon performs an endoscopic assessment of the duodenal laterally spreading lesion, which is near circumferential and involves the major papilla. Using a Carlock needle with a duodenoscope, a submucosal injection of succinylated gelatin, indigo carmine, and adrenaline is administered beneath the lesion. A 15mm oval snare with a 0.47mm thickness wire is used for sequential mucosal resection of the lesion, employing standard EMR polypectomy settings on the ERBI electro generator.

The surgeon uses the edge of a previous resection defect as an anchor for the snare, ensuring controlled capture of the tissue. The mobility of the captured tissue is assessed relative to adjacent tissue, confirming no muscularis propria involvement. Sequential endoscopic mucosal resection of the laterally spreading lesion is performed using electrocautery.

Following resection, bleeding is observed from a transected arteriole. The area is irrigated with a triple lumen synchrotome, revealing the source. A coagulation grasper is used to capture and obliterate the vessel with soft coagulating current, stopping the bleeding. The sequential endoscopic mucosal resection of the laterally spreading component is completed, leaving the ampullary adenoma. The adenoma is captured en bloc with a snare, which is fully tightened for transection using the specified EMR settings.

A five French single pigtail plastic pancreatic stent is placed prophylactically, followed by the insertion of a ten millimeter by six centimeter fully covered metal biliary stent to prevent microperforation and bleeding.

The patient was admitted to the ward, kept nearby mouth, and commenced on an intravenous PPI and octreotide infusion. The patient developed hematemesis on day two, but this resolved spontaneously. Her postoperative course was otherwise unremarkable. She received two units of Pax Cells, and do not require repeat endoscopic or radiologic intervention prior to discharge.

We demonstrate through this case that endoscopic resection of large duodenal laterally spreading lesions is feasible and can be curative. Comorbid patients may preferentially benefit from endoscopic resection as this carries lower morbidity and mortality compared to surgery. However, such resection should only be performed by expert endoscopists in a tertiary centre where adequate supports exist.
